title: Leviticus: Day of Atonement
objective: Deepen biblical-theological understanding of leviticus: day of atonement and its place in redemptive history.

required_passages.text: Selected key passages for this unit segment, read in full during lesson delivery.

segments.audio_script: Today we study leviticus: day of atonement and trace God's covenant faithfulness.
Listen to the assigned passage and note covenant themes, divine actions, and human response.
We place this text in its historical setting, canonical location, and literary flow.
We analyze theological meaning, covenant development, and connections to the wider biblical story.
Key themes include God's holiness, covenant faithfulness, judgment and mercy, and redemptive purpose.

Review question: What happened to the second goat on the Day of Atonement, the scapegoat? Answer: the high priest laid both hands on it and confessed all Israel's sins over it, then it was sent into the wilderness carrying the sins away.
segments.correct_answer: the high priest confessed Israel's sins over it and sent it into the wilderness to carry sins away
acceptable_variants: it was driven into the wilderness bearing Israel's sins
the scapegoat carried the sins of israel away from the camp
azazel — the goat sent bearing the people's guilt

segments.audio_script: Carry this lesson into prayer, worship, and careful Scripture reading as we continue the course.

vocabulary.definition: Yom Kippur — the annual day when the high priest alone entered the Most Holy Place to make atonement for all Israel's sins through blood sacrifice and the scapegoat ritual
The second goat on Yom Kippur over which Israel's sins were confessed; it was sent into the wilderness symbolizing the removal of sin from the community
